topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard felt thin and cheap under my fingertips as I pulled the envelope from the slot. It was an unsigned birthday card, stark white with a hastily scribbled, looping script that looked distinctly amateur. My jaw tightened, the muscles in my neck corded with a sudden, sharp tension. I stared at the blank space where a name should have been, a slow, burning heat rising in my chest. Who on earth thought they had the right to send something so utterly dismissive? I crumpled the paper slightly, the sound a dry rustle in the quiet hallway. I didn't bother putting it in the recycling; I just shoved it deep into the bottom of the junk drawer, a silent acknowledgment of the sheer audacity of the gesture.

[story 2]

I picked up the card, my knuckles turning white against the glossy surface. The handwriting was aggressively cheerful, all bright pink ink and overly enthusiastic flourishes. A wave of profound annoyance washed over me, making my stomach clench. It was clearly meant for me, yet the absence of a name felt like a deliberate snub. I reread the vague, saccharine message—something about 'celebrating another trip around the sun'—and a tight, humorless smile touched my lips. I let out a slow, controlled breath, trying to smooth the agitated tremor running through my hands. I folded it with meticulous, almost angry precision, then dropped it into the wastebasket with a deliberate, sharp clatter.

[story 3]

When I saw the card, I stopped mid-stride. The unexpected presence of it in the mailbox had instantly soured the afternoon. The paper felt flimsy, and the script, while decorative, seemed to mock the occasion. My eyes scanned the card, searching for any hint of who this person thought they were addressing, but found only empty space. A low, simmering frustration began to bubble up, making my shoulders hunch up toward my ears. I didn't want to acknowledge the sender; the very act of receiving something so carelessly presented felt like a profound slight to my expectations. With a frustrated sigh that was almost a hiss, I dropped the card onto the nearest surface, the motion conveying a distinct sense of dismissal.
